decisions.issue: UI Overhaul Architecture: Incremental Enhancement vs Layout Redesign + Incremental vs Full Redesign
decisions.recommendation: Option B: Layout Redesign + Incremental. Redesign the shell (navigation, layout containers, debug panel infrastructure) first in 2-3 tasks, then incrementally add features (CRUD forms, detail views, real-time) within the new shell. This gives the structural foundation needed for the 7 sub-ideas without the risk of a full ground-up rewrite.
decisions.reasoning: Option A fails because the current flat tab navigation cannot scale to detail pages, slide-over panels, and a persistent debug console — adding more tabs makes UX worse. Option C is unnecessary because the existing component patterns (Card, Badge, StatusFilter), Zustand store architecture, and API client are solid — they need extension, not replacement. Option B threads the needle: fix the structural limitation (layout/nav), then reuse everything that works (stores, API client, card components, WebSocket infrastructure).
alternatives: Option A: Incremental Enhancement — add features to existing tab-based layout one by one
Option C: Full Redesign — rethink everything from scratch with new architecture
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED Option B. User decisions on open questions: (1) Detail pages via Next.js dynamic routes (not modal overlays), (2) Debug panel pushes content up (not overlay), (3) Command palette (Cmd+K) included in layout phase (not deferred).

findings.detail: entityStore.ts is well-designed: generic CRUD helpers, WebSocket event mapping, sequence-based stale response prevention, optimistic removes. api.ts has typed wrappers for all 72 endpoints. types.ts matches Pydantic models. This foundation is solid and should NOT be rewritten.

Root layout.tsx has Sidebar (2 items: Dashboard, Projects) + main content area. Project layout.tsx has 12 horizontal tabs in a single row. Adding detail pages (task/T-001, decision/D-003, etc.) would create 20+ tabs or require nested routing that the flat tab bar cannot express. This is the core structural limitation.

ForgeWebSocket class and entityStore.handleEvent are fully implemented. The gap is not in infrastructure but in UI consumption: lists don't visually update because page components fetch on mount but don't re-render on WS events. The entityStore already handles WS → state updates; the issue is that list pages fetch once and filter client-side without reacting to store changes. This is actually a minor fix, not architectural.

8 entity cards (TaskCard, DecisionCard, ObjectiveCard, etc.) follow a consistent pattern: border, hover effect, badges, tags. These can be reused as list items AND as summary cards in detail views. SuggestionPanel, ContextView, DebugMonitor are substantial self-contained components. ExecutionStream exists. All are reusable.

Missing: 6 create forms, 6 detail pages, settings page, gates UI, debug panel (vs current debug page), API inspector, real-time toast notifications, command palette. None of these require architectural changes to stores or API client — they need layout changes and new page/component files.

options.name: Option A: Incremental Enhancement
options.description: Add missing features to existing tab-based UI one by one. No layout changes.
pros: Lowest initial risk — working code stays working
Each feature can be shipped independently
No wasted work on layout that might not be right
cons: Tab bar already has 12 items — adding detail pages, settings, and execution views makes it unnavigable (18+ tabs)
No place to put persistent debug console panel
Contextual navigation (T-001 links to D-003) requires router patterns the flat layout does not support
3-click target impossible: tab + scroll + click = already 3 with no detail view
Accumulates UX debt as each feature is wedged into wrong container
options.verdict: REJECTED — structural limitation in layout makes this approach hit a ceiling at ~50% of the goal

options.name: Option B: Layout Redesign + Incremental
options.description: Phase 1: Redesign the shell (sidebar with project entities as tree, breadcrumb navigation, resizable debug panel, command palette). Phase 2+: Add features incrementally within the new shell.
pros: Fixes the structural bottleneck (navigation) without touching working code
New layout supports: detail views via nested routes, debug panel via resizable bottom panel, command palette via global overlay
Existing stores, API client, cards, hooks all carry forward unchanged
Can be done in 2-3 tasks (layout shell, breadcrumb nav, debug panel container) before feature work begins
Each subsequent feature task is simpler because it drops into a prepared layout
Enables 3-click target: sidebar entity group → item → action
cons: Layout shell task is a bigger single commit — harder to code review
Risk of bikeshedding on layout details
Must define the layout API contract upfront (slot names, resize behavior, route structure)
Sidebar tree view needs to load entity counts — 8 API calls on project load (already happening today on dashboard)

options.name: Option C: Full Redesign
options.description: Start from scratch: new component library, new state management pattern, new routing structure, new styling approach.
pros: Can optimize everything from first principles
No legacy patterns to work around
Could adopt newer patterns (RSC for data fetching, server actions for mutations)
cons: Throws away ~2500 lines of working, well-tested code (stores, API client, cards, hooks, DnD)
3-5x longer timeline with no intermediate value delivery
High risk of scope creep — 'while we are rewriting, let us also...'
RSC patterns conflict with standalone output mode (used for Docker deployment)
Second-system effect: new architecture may not be better, just different
Zustand store is already well-architected — replacing it with React Query or similar gains nothing
options.verdict: REJECTED — throwing away working code with no clear architectural benefit

open_questions: Should the sidebar show entity counts live (WebSocket updated) or only on navigation?
Should detail pages use Next.js dynamic routes (/projects/[slug]/tasks/[id]) or modal overlays?
How should the debug console panel interact with the main scroll area — overlay or push content up?
Should command palette (Cmd+K) be added in the layout phase or deferred?
evidence_refs: forge-web/stores/entityStore.ts — well-designed, 393 lines, full CRUD + WS

decisions.issue: Performance degradation with large entity lists (100+ tasks, 50+ decisions) in Zustand flat array store
decisions.recommendation: Mitigate by implementing virtual scrolling for lists exceeding 50 items and adding pagination parameters to fetchEntities. Zustand itself handles 1000+ items fine — the risk is React re-renders from array updates, not store performance.
decisions.reasoning: Current entityStore stores all items in flat arrays. WebSocket events trigger full slice replacement via set(). With 100+ tasks, each WS event causes React to diff the entire list. This is O(n) per event, which at 10 events/second could cause jank. Virtual scrolling (react-window or similar) limits rendered DOM nodes. Pagination limits fetched data. Together they keep the UI responsive.
alternatives: Accept and do nothing — works until entity counts exceed ~200, then degrades
Move to normalized entity store (id-keyed map) — more complex, fixes diffing but adds lookup overhead for rendering
Server-side pagination only — limits data but breaks client-side filtering
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Add limit/offset params to fetchEntities (already supported by most API endpoints). 2. For lists > 50 items, lazy-render with react-window. 3. For WebSocket updates on large lists, batch state updates with unstable_batchedUpdates or Zustand's built-in batching. 4. Profile with React DevTools Profiler before optimizing — premature optimization is likely.
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Pagination + virtual scrolling when needed. Profile before optimizing.

decisions.issue: fetch() monkey-patching for API Inspector may conflict with Next.js internal fetch behavior
decisions.recommendation: Use a custom API client interceptor pattern instead of global fetch() monkey-patching. Wrap the existing request() function in api.ts with a logging layer that feeds the debug console, rather than patching window.fetch globally.
decisions.reasoning: Next.js 14 App Router uses fetch() internally for data caching, revalidation, and RSC. Monkey-patching window.fetch() could: (1) capture internal Next.js requests that are not user API calls, creating noise, (2) interfere with Next.js fetch deduplication and caching, (3) break in production standalone builds where fetch may be polyfilled differently. The safer approach is to intercept at the application layer (the request() function in api.ts) rather than the platform layer (global fetch). This captures all Forge API calls without touching Next.js internals.
alternatives: Global fetch() monkey-patch with URL filtering (filter out /_next/ and internal URLs)
Service Worker interceptor (cleaner but adds deployment complexity)
Browser DevTools Network tab (no custom UI needed, but not integrated into Forge)
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Add an interceptor layer to the request() function in api.ts that emits request/response data to a debug store. 2. Create useDebugStore with a circular buffer (last 500 requests). 3. Debug console reads from this store. 4. No global fetch patching needed. 5. WebSocket events already flow through the existing ForgeWebSocket dispatch — add a debug tap there too.
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Use api.ts request() interceptor, NOT global fetch() monkey-patch.

decisions.issue: Layout redesign may break existing pages during transition period
decisions.recommendation: Implement the layout redesign as a progressive enhancement: new layout components are added alongside existing ones, with a feature flag or route-based toggle. Existing tab navigation remains functional while new sidebar is built. Cut over once new layout is validated.
decisions.reasoning: The project layout (layout.tsx) is the single point of change. Modifying it breaks all 12 entity pages simultaneously. A progressive approach: (1) create new layout component (ProjectLayoutV2), (2) wire it up in a parallel route or behind an environment variable, (3) validate all pages render correctly in new layout, (4) swap default layout, (5) remove old layout. This keeps the existing UI functional throughout development.
alternatives: Big bang swap — change layout.tsx directly, fix all pages in one commit
Branch-based development — develop new layout on a branch, merge when ready
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Build new layout components in components/layout/ without touching existing layout.tsx. 2. Use environment variable NEXT_PUBLIC_LAYOUT_V2=true to toggle. 3. Test each page in both layouts. 4. Once validated, make V2 the default. 5. Remove old layout code.
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Progressive enhancement with feature flag during transition.

decisions.issue: State management explosion: entityStore (393 LOC) becomes monolithic god-store when 6 new CRUD forms + inline editing + form draft state + toast state are added, projected to exceed 1000 LOC with tightly coupled state logic
decisions.recommendation: Split entityStore into per-entity stores with shared factory pattern before starting I-003. Extract form state to component-local or dedicated form store. Keep centralized WS event dispatcher that routes to individual stores.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=5 I=4 V=3 D=4 R=2 Composite=29. Current store handles 10 entity types with CRUD + WS. Adding 6 create forms, 4+ inline editors, optimistic updates, toast, and filter state pushes it past maintainability threshold. Monolithic store makes WS race conditions (D-007) harder to isolate. Per-entity stores keep each under 100 LOC.
alternatives: Keep single store with middleware layers
Use TanStack Query for server state + Zustand for UI-only state
Use React context per entity type
decisions.mitigation_plan: Before I-003: (1) Create store factory function for common CRUD helpers, (2) Split into per-entity stores (taskStore, decisionStore...), (3) Keep WS dispatcher centralized routing to stores, (4) Form draft state in component-local state only, (5) Add lastSyncedAt timestamp per entity for conflict resolution
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Split entityStore into per-entity stores with factory pattern in Phase 0.

decisions.issue: Form validation divergence: frontend TypeScript types silently desync from backend Pydantic schemas. Evidence: IdeaUpdate allows COMMITTED status but backend rejects it; ChangeAction includes 'verify' but backend doesn't; 14+ Record<string,unknown> escape hatches in types.ts
decisions.recommendation: Generate TypeScript types from FastAPI OpenAPI spec using openapi-typescript. Add zod schemas for frontend validation that mirror Pydantic constraints. Implement field-level 422 error mapping in forms.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=4 I=4 V=2 D=5 R=3 Composite=26. Detectability=5 (hardest) because mismatches only surface as 422 errors after user fills form. No shared schema definition exists. T-044 added backend status transition guards with no frontend counterpart. Combined with no-test risk (D-008), these ship undetected.
alternatives: Manually sync types on each backend change
Shared JSON Schema as single source of truth
Runtime validation using zod mirroring Pydantic
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Add openapi-typescript to devDeps, (2) Generate types from /openapi.json, (3) Replace manual types.ts, (4) Add field-level 422 error mapping, (5) Create validation.ts with frontend status transition guards linked to backend counterparts
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Generate types from OpenAPI spec + zod schemas for frontend validation.

decisions.issue: WebSocket race conditions: three competing data update paths (manual fetch, optimistic update, WS event) produce duplicate/stale items. Current dedup (ID check) fails with optimistic updates using temporary data. Dashboard fires 8 fetches on mount compounding the issue.
decisions.recommendation: Adopt server-is-truth pattern for creates (saving indicator, no optimistic add). For updates: optimistic with rollback, suppress matching WS event by ID for 2s. Add dedup window tracking recently-mutated entity IDs.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=4 I=4 V=4 D=4 R=2 Composite=26. entityStore handleEvent dedup (lines 373-377) works for known IDs but breaks with optimistic data. removeTask (lines 253-265) is only optimistic op currently. Adding 6+ entity create/update optimistic ops multiplies race surface. Amplified by R-001 (monolithic store makes isolation harder).
alternatives: Use mutation IDs to correlate API response with WS event
Implement CRDT-like last-write-wins with vector clocks
Abandon real-time updates, use polling
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Creates: show saving spinner, no optimistic add, wait for API+WS, (2) Updates: optimistic with rollback, suppress matching WS for 2s, (3) Deletes: keep existing pattern, (4) Track recently-mutated IDs in 5s dedup window, (5) In multi-agent mode only suppress own user's mutations
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Server-is-truth for creates, optimistic+rollback for updates, 5s dedup window.

decisions.issue: No testing infrastructure: forge-web has 0 test files, 0 test runner, 0 testing dependencies. UI overhaul adds 20-30 new components with complex state interactions verified only by npm run build. T-038 through T-049 (12 bugs) were found by manual deep-verify, not automated tests.
decisions.recommendation: Add vitest + @testing-library/react before overhaul. Write unit tests for validation logic and store operations. 1 test per new component (renders without crash). Do NOT write snapshot or DOM structure tests.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=4 I=4 V=1 D=4 R=3 Composite=24. Velocity=1 (slow onset) but combined with form validation divergence (D-006), regression risk compounds over weeks. 12 existing bugs found by manual review demonstrates build-only validation is insufficient. Testing form validation functions has highest ROI.
alternatives: Playwright E2E tests instead
Storybook visual regression
Continue build-only with periodic deep-verify
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/jest-dom jsdom, (2) Configure vitest.config.ts, (3) 1 test per store slice, (4) 1 test per validation function, (5) 1 smoke test per form component, (6) Add 'test' script to package.json
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Add vitest + testing-library in Phase 0 before overhaul.

decisions.issue: Backend API gaps: Settings page needs PATCH /projects/{slug} (doesn't exist), Dashboard activity feed needs recent-events endpoint (doesn't exist), Bulk operations need batch endpoints (don't exist). Discovery mid-implementation blocks progress.
decisions.recommendation: Audit required API calls against endpoints before each sub-idea. Prioritize adding PATCH /projects/{slug} and GET /projects/{slug}/activity. Defer bulk operations to v2.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=3 I=4 V=4 D=3 R=3 Composite=22. api.ts projects object (lines 173-178) has no update method. No activity endpoint exists. Velocity=4 because gap discovery immediately blocks the frontend developer. Reversibility=3 because adding backend endpoints requires testing and Docker rebuild.
alternatives: Build UI with mock data, add endpoints later
Only build features existing endpoints support
Add all missing endpoints as prerequisite
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Before each sub-idea, list all API calls needed, (2) Check against forge-api routers, (3) Missing endpoints: add backend task as blocker, (4) Settings: add PATCH /projects/{slug} first, (5) Dashboard activity: derive from entity lists rather than new endpoint
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Audit endpoints before each sub-idea. Add missing ones as backend tasks.

decisions.issue: Debug Console performance: capturing all API payloads + rendering JSON trees in bottom panel causes UI jank. Dashboard fires 8 fetchEntities on mount. DebugMonitor.tsx already 641 LOC with complex nested CollapsibleSection components.
decisions.recommendation: Capture metadata only (URL, status, timing) by default. Lazy-load payloads on click. Use circular buffer (max 200 entries). Virtualize the session list. Use requestIdleCallback for non-critical rendering.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=4 I=3 V=5 D=3 R=2 Composite=22. Velocity=5 (hits instantly on any page with API calls). Dashboard fires 8 concurrent fetches = 8 capture operations + 8 renders simultaneously. Combined with D-003 risk of fetch monkey-patching interfering with Next.js internals.
alternatives: Use browser DevTools instead
Capture to IndexedDB, render on demand
Only capture when panel is open
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Ring buffer max 200 entries, (2) Virtualized list for visible entries, (3) Lazy-load response bodies on click, (4) Use api.ts request() interceptor not global fetch patch, (5) FPS counter in dev mode to detect jank
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Ring buffer, lazy-load payloads, virtualized list, api.ts interceptor.

decisions.issue: Missing form library: no react-hook-form in package.json. Building 6 complex entity forms (5-15 fields each: text, select, multi-select, KeyResult[] arrays, relations) + inline editing from scratch estimated at 2000+ LOC vs 800 LOC with library.
decisions.recommendation: Install react-hook-form + @hookform/resolvers + zod. Create reusable FormField component. Build first form (Task) then replicate pattern.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=4 I=3 V=3 D=2 R=2 Composite=19. High probability because scope demands 6 forms minimum. Impact moderate: slow velocity, not system failure. Each entity has unique field types (KeyResult arrays, entity references, enum selects). Hand-coding form state, validation, dirty tracking for all is 60% more code than with library.
alternatives: Formik
Build minimal custom form hooks
Native HTML validation only
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) npm install react-hook-form @hookform/resolvers zod, (2) Create lib/schemas/ with zod schemas per entity, (3) Build reusable FormField wrapping RHF Controller, (4) Apply to Task form first, replicate to other 5
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Install react-hook-form + zod. Create reusable FormField component.

decisions.issue: Scope creep via UI perfectionism: 8 sub-ideas (I-002 through I-008) will expand during implementation. Dashboard widget requests, extra filters, animations, responsive tweaks. Prior velocity (50 tasks in 14 hours) creates expectation that 'one more thing' is cheap.
decisions.recommendation: Lock scope per sub-idea BEFORE starting: write explicit acceptance criteria and 'NOT in this iteration' list. Time-box each sub-idea to 3 days max. If blocked or expanding, stop and reassess.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=5 I=3 V=2 D=3 R=2 Composite=22. Probability=5 near-certain for any UI project. UI work has longer feedback loops than backend (visual review, adjustment, cross-page consistency). Feeds into reinforcing loop: scope creep -> state explosion (D-005) -> WS races (D-007) -> no tests catch it (D-008) -> quality decline -> burnout.
alternatives: Strict 2-day time-box per sub-idea
User acceptance testing after each
Cut low-KR-impact sub-ideas entirely
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) For each sub-idea write AC BEFORE starting, (2) Anything not in AC = 'defer to v2', (3) Review against O-005 KRs, (4) 3-day max time-box, (5) If expanding: stop, record as decision, reassess
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Lock AC per sub-idea, 3-day time-box, anything outside AC = v2.

decisions.issue: Deep 5D Risk Analysis for I-001 (Forge Platform UI Overhaul): 15 risks across 5 lenses (Technical, Organizational, Temporal, Dependency, Knowledge), 6 uncertainties, 6 risk interactions mapped, mitigations with cobra-effect review
decisions.recommendation: Execute sub-ideas in risk-informed order: I-003 (Forms) -> I-004 (Detail Views) -> I-007 (Real-time) -> I-002 (Dashboard) -> I-005 (Board) -> I-008 (Debug Console) -> I-006 (Settings). Address infrastructure (store split, form lib, tests) BEFORE feature work.
decisions.reasoning: 5D scoring identified top 5: State Management Explosion (29), Form Validation Divergence (26), WebSocket Race Conditions (26), No Testing Infrastructure (24), Backend API Gaps (22). Central reinforcing loop: scope creep -> state explosion -> WS races -> no tests catch it -> quality decline -> burnout -> more shortcuts. Breaking the loop requires: (1) split store, (2) add tests, (3) add form lib BEFORE starting feature tasks.

findings.detail: 76 files, 10338 LOC, 0 tests, 0 form library, 0 DnD library in deps
393 LOC already at complexity limit. Adding 6 CRUD forms + inline editing + form draft state pushes past 1000 LOC threshold
14+ Record<string,unknown> escape hatches in types.ts. IdeaUpdate allows COMMITTED, ChangeAction includes verify — both rejected by backend
3 competing paths: manual fetch, optimistic update, WS event. Dedup only works when IDs match exactly. Dashboard fires 8 concurrent fetches on mount
6 amplification pairs identified. Central feedback loop: scope creep -> state complexity -> WS races -> untested bugs -> quality decline -> burnout
6 unknowns: Next.js 14 with 30+ dynamic routes; WS reliability at scale; DnD Kit dual context; CLI vs UI usage split; debug session list scalability; DAG readability at 50+ tasks
Security (XSS/CSRF), accessibility (WCAG), mobile, i18n, deployment infra, LLM costs, browser compatibility, data migration

open_questions: Should TanStack Query replace Zustand for server state management?
Is generating types from OpenAPI spec feasible given current backend structure?
Should Kanban board use same DnD provider as entity palette or separate?
What is actual user workflow split between CLI and UI?

decisions.issue: O-008 Objectives Module Overhaul: KR schema, LLM enrichment, guidelines, knowledge, dependencies — implementation approach
decisions.recommendation: Phased approach: Phase 1 — descriptive KR (add optional description field, backward compat) + guideline_ids + relations fields. Phase 2 — LLM enrichment (POST /ai/suggest-kr, side panel with Apply buttons). Phase 3 — graph visualization. Use existing patterns: additive optional fields (like knowledge_ids), heuristic+LLM hybrid (like suggest-guidelines), typed relations (like ideas.relations).
decisions.reasoning: 5 option maps explored. KR schema: Option A (extended, additive) wins over union type or wrapper — minimal migration, backward compat. Frontend: multi-stage (light form + rich detail) over complex modal. LLM: side panel + batch suggestions over inline auto-fill or wizard. Guidelines: hybrid scopes+explicit over pure scope-only. Dependencies: typed relations (matches ideas) over simple depends_on.
alternatives: KR union type (MetricKR | DescriptiveKR) — cleaner types but breaking change
LLM wizard modal — better quality but requires WebSocket streaming
Junction table for guideline assignment — more scalable but over-engineered

findings.detail: Current: metric+baseline+target+current (numeric only). Add optional description field + success_criteria + assumptions. Validation: either metric OR description required.
Existing /ai/suggest-* pattern with heuristic scoring. New endpoint: POST /ai/suggest-kr with mock mode. Side panel UI with Apply buttons per suggestion.
Add guideline_ids field (like knowledge_ids). Final set = union(scopes-loaded) ∪ explicit. UI: auto-loaded list + Customize expander.
Add relations field matching ideas pattern. Types: depends_on, related_to, supersedes, duplicates. Cycle detection on save.

open_questions: Should KR description fully replace metric or always coexist?
Should LLM enrichment use mock mode first or wire real providers immediately?
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Phased approach confirmed: Phase 1 (KR schema + guidelines + relations), Phase 2 (LLM enrichment), Phase 3 (graph viz). All option map recommendations approved.

decisions.issue: O-008 Risk Register: 8 risks across backward compat, schema migration, LLM integration, API contracts, UI complexity, circular deps, performance, scope creep
decisions.recommendation: GO with mandatory scope segmentation. Address T1 risks (scope, backward compat, API contracts) during planning. T2 risks (LLM contracts, form decomposition, cycle detection, lazy loading) during implementation. T3 (migration) during validation.
decisions.reasoning: Average composite score 3.3/5 (HIGH-MEDIUM). Top risks: R-4 API contracts (3.6), R-7 performance (3.6), R-3 LLM integration (3.4), R-8 scope creep (3.4). Reinforcing loop: scope → UI complexity → LLM integration → scope. Break with: MVP phases + form decomposition.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Phase O-008 into MVP (descriptive KR + guidelines) and follow-up (LLM + deps). Dual-mode KR validation. Optional new fields with defaults. Lazy loading for multi-select dropdowns.
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. GO decision confirmed. All mitigations approved: phased scope, dual-mode KR, lazy loading, form decomposition.

decisions.issue: O-008 Architecture: 4 ADRs — KR schema design, LLM enrichment pattern, guideline assignment model, objective dependencies
decisions.recommendation: ADR-1: Optional description field (additive). ADR-2: Side panel + batch suggestions (stateless, existing /ai/ pattern). ADR-3: Scopes + optional guideline_ids (union). ADR-4: Typed relations matching ideas pattern.
decisions.reasoning: Each ADR was evaluated against 3 options. Recommendations favor: backward compatibility, pattern consistency with existing entities (ideas, knowledge), incremental adoption, and minimal migration cost. Component diagram shows ObjectiveForm with KREditor, KRSuggestionsPanel, GuidelineAssignment, KnowledgeLinker, and RelationEditor.
alternatives: ADR-1 union type
ADR-2 WebSocket wizard
ADR-3 junction table
ADR-4 simple depends_on
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. All 4 ADRs approved: ADR-1 optional description, ADR-2 side panel suggestions, ADR-3 scopes+guideline_ids union, ADR-4 typed relations.

decisions.issue: UI must preserve existing Forge CLI workflow operations — every entity action available in CLI must be exposed in UI
decisions.recommendation: Each entity type in UI must have an Actions menu exposing CLI-equivalent operations. Objectives: discover, update KR progress, achieve/abandon. Ideas: explore, approve, reject, commit. Tasks: claim/next, complete, gates check. Decisions: close, defer, reopen. Lessons: promote to guideline/knowledge. Knowledge: link, version, deprecate.
decisions.reasoning: User explicitly stated that the existing Forge core process (objectives → discover, ideas → explore/approve/commit, tasks → next/complete, etc.) must be preserved in the UI. The UI enhances the process with visual management but does NOT replace CLI operations. Every CLI command should have a UI equivalent accessible from the entity detail page.
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Hard constraint confirmed: UI must expose CLI-equivalent operations for every entity type via Actions menu.

decisions.issue: O-009 Skills Management: storage model, global routing, TESLint integration, versioning, and SKILL.md editor approach
decisions.recommendation: Option C: Full DB storage (SKILL.md as TEXT, evals as JSONB, resource metadata as JSONB) with on-demand temp dir extraction for TESLint. Global routing at /api/v1/skills (no project slug). Update-in-place for v1 (no versioning). Plain textarea editor (no CodeMirror/Monaco in v1).
decisions.reasoning: 3 options evaluated. Option B (hybrid DB+filesystem) rejected — deployment complexity not justified. Option A (full DB) and Option C (DB + temp dir for linting) are essentially the same, with C specifying the TESLint integration pattern. DB storage is consistent with all other entities. TESLint is stateless — temp dir extract→lint→cleanup works without persistent filesystem. Global routing is architecturally correct — skills don't belong to projects.
alternatives: Option B: Hybrid DB + filesystem — SKILL.md on disk, metadata in DB
Option A: Full DB without TESLint integration plan
Option D: Virtual 'global' project to scope skills under existing /projects/{slug}/ routes

findings.detail: Full DB: SKILL.md as TEXT (~40KB max), evals as JSONB, resources as JSONB metadata. TESLint extract on demand only (lint/promote). No persistent filesystem needed.
Skills router at /api/v1/skills — first non-project entity. JWT auth works on header, not path. Separate from _helpers.py project-scoped helpers. ID prefix: S-001, S-002.

Backend service creates temp dir: /tmp/teslint-{uuid}/.claude/skills/{name}/SKILL.md, runs python -m teslint --format json, parses output, cleans up. 3 touchpoints: inline editor (on-demand), promotion gate (automatic), API endpoint POST /skills/{id}/lint.
v1: update-in-place with updated_at. promotion_history tracks lifecycle events. No SKILL.md version history. Defer to v2 based on need.
v1: plain textarea with monospace font. Lint button triggers TESLintReport component. No syntax highlighting. No live preview. CodeMirror/Monaco deferred to v2.

open_questions: Should bundled resources (scripts/, references/) support file upload in v1 or be metadata-only?
Should skills have an 'owner' field for multi-user environments?
How should the frontend handle the new global /skills route alongside /projects/{slug} routes?
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Full DB storage, global routing, TESLint subprocess, plain textarea editor. Bundled resources deferred to v2 (metadata-only in v1). Owner field and global route integration to be resolved during /plan.

decisions.issue: O-009 Feasibility Assessment: 10-dimension scoring for Skills Management module
decisions.recommendation: GO — average 4.5/5, no binding constraints. Adjusted timeline: 7-10 days (with deep-verify overhead). Most optimistic assumption: TESLint subprocess in Docker works seamlessly; fallback: manual YAML validation.
decisions.reasoning: All 10 dimensions score 4-5/5. Technical=5 (factory pattern for new entities). Knowledge=5 (Phase 2 lessons directly applicable). Scale=5 (low-cardinality entity). Only moderate scores: Temporal=4 (TESLint integration adds 1-2 days), Compositional=4 (global entity is new pattern), Resource=4 (single developer), Cognitive=4 (TESLint subprocess is only novelty). Reference class: Phase 2 averaged 45min per task for similar entity CRUD work.

findings.detail: GO — 4.5/5 average, no binding constraints, reference class validates timeline
Phase 2: 19 tasks in ~14h. Per-entity CRUD: ~1h. Adjusted for deep-verify (1.5x): 7-10 days
TESLint subprocess in Docker backend. Fallback: regex-based YAML frontmatter validation

decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. GO decision confirmed. 4.5/5 average, no binding constraints. Timeline: 7-10 days.

decisions.issue: TESLint scanner requires specific path structure (.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md) — temp dir must match exactly
decisions.recommendation: Create temp dir with exact structure: /tmp/teslint-{uuid}/.claude/skills/{skill_name}/SKILL.md. Verify with integration test during development. Add .teslintrc.json to temp dir root if skill has custom config.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=3 I=3 V=4 D=4 R=1 Composite=18. TESLint scanner discovers files matching .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md pattern. Wrong structure → scanner finds nothing → returns 0 findings (false pass). Detectability=4 because false pass is silent. Velocity=4 because it blocks immediately. Reversibility=1 because path fix is trivial.
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Create temp dir: /tmp/teslint-{uuid}/.claude/skills/{name}/SKILL.md, (2) If skill has .teslintrc.json in metadata, write to temp dir root, (3) Verify with test lint during teslint.py development, (4) Log actual findings count to detect false passes (0 findings on non-empty SKILL.md = suspect)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Mitigation plan approved: temp dir with .claude/skills/{name}/SKILL.md structure, findings count logging, integration test during development.

decisions.issue: Global routing (/api/v1/skills) may conflict with per-project middleware — auth, dependency injection, event emission
decisions.recommendation: Create skills router independently from per-project routers. Verify JWT auth works without slug parameter. Extract shared helpers (auth, event bus) into reusable dependencies not tied to project context.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=2 I=4 V=4 D=3 R=2 Composite=17. FastAPI middleware stack and dependency injection may assume {slug} in path. Skills router has no slug. Impact=4 because it breaks entire Skills API. Velocity=4 because it fails on first request. However, Probability=2 because JWT auth uses Authorization header (not path), and FastAPI dependencies are per-router (not global middleware).
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Review main.py app.include_router() — verify no global slug-dependent middleware, (2) Create skills.py router with own auth dependency (reuse get_current_user), (3) For events: create skills-specific event types (skill.created, skill.promoted), (4) Test first endpoint before building full CRUD
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Mitigation plan approved: separate router, reuse JWT auth, skills-specific event types, test first endpoint.

decisions.issue: Editing ACTIVE skill affects all tasks referencing it — no versioning, no rollback, no change notification
decisions.recommendation: v1: update-in-place with updated_at timestamp. Document behavior clearly. v2: optional version history. promotion_history already captures lifecycle events.
decisions.reasoning: 5D Score: P=3 I=3 V=2 D=3 R=3 Composite=17. User may edit ACTIVE skill without realizing tasks reference it. No diff, no rollback. However: skills are low-cardinality (10-100), edits are intentional (not accidental), and task.skill_id is a reference (tasks don't snapshot skill content). Reversibility=3 because SKILL.md content can be manually restored but requires remembering previous version.
decisions.mitigation_plan: (1) Show 'Used by N tasks' count on edit page as awareness aid, (2) Require confirmation when editing ACTIVE skill, (3) updated_at timestamp for audit trail, (4) Defer version history to v2
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Mitigation plan approved: 'Used by N tasks' count, confirmation dialog on ACTIVE edit, update-in-place v1, defer versioning to v2.

decisions.issue: O-009 Architecture: 5 ADRs — storage model, global routing, TESLint subprocess, versioning strategy, promotion override
decisions.recommendation: ADR-1: Full DB storage (TEXT + JSONB). ADR-2: Global /api/v1/skills routing. ADR-3: TESLint as subprocess with temp dir. ADR-4: Update-in-place for v1. ADR-5: Force override on promotion with audit trail.
decisions.reasoning: Each ADR evaluated against 2-3 alternatives. Recommendations favor: consistency with existing entity patterns, deployment simplicity, correct domain modeling (skills are global, not per-project), and pragmatic v1 scope. Component diagram: SkillRouter → TESLintService (subprocess), SkillStore (Zustand factory), SkillsPage/SkillDetailPage/SkillForm (React). New DB table: skills (no project_id FK).
alternatives: Hybrid filesystem storage
Virtual global project for routing
TESLint as library import
Full version history in v1
Strict promotion without override
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. All 5 ADRs approved: (1) Full DB storage TEXT+JSONB, (2) Global /api/v1/skills routing, (3) TESLint subprocess with temp dir, (4) Update-in-place for v1, (5) Force override on promotion with audit trail.

decisions.issue: LLM Core Integration — full architecture exploration for O-010/O-011
decisions.recommendation: Global chat endpoint with context routing, WebSocket streaming, agentic tool-use loop, hybrid permissions, JSONB for multi-file skills, env vars for API keys
decisions.reasoning: 6 architectural options explored across 6 decision dimensions. Existing infrastructure (WS, Zustand, provider adapters, contracts) is 95% ready — gap is ~6-8 hours of wiring. Recommended architecture maximizes reuse of existing patterns while enabling LLM as core platform capability.
findings: Provider adapters are FULLY IMPLEMENTED in core/llm/providers/ — only need activation in forge-api
AI Router has 6 endpoints ALL returning MOCK data — LLM_MODE='mock' in ai.py line 23
Contract system is 85% complete — base classes done, some contracts may be stubs
Context Assembly Engine is COMPLETE with 9 prioritized sections and token budgeting
WebSocket infrastructure handles streaming — ExecutionStream pattern already works
SkillEditor.tsx (670 lines) needs 3rd panel for chat (currently 2-column: editor + tabs)
Gap to live LLM: provider init in lifespan, replace _mock_llm_call, implement tool executor
open_questions: Are contract implementations in knowledge.py/planning.py/analysis.py/review.py complete or stubs?
What is the optimal token limit per chat session?
Should tool call preview require user confirmation before execution?
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. WebSocket streaming, global chat endpoint, agentic loop with tool-use. User accepted all recommendations.

decisions.issue: LLM Core Architecture — 6 ADRs defining system design
decisions.recommendation: Accept all 6 ADRs as documented in research/deep-architect-llm-core-integration.md
decisions.reasoning: ADR-1: Global chat endpoint over per-module (max reuse, single permission path). ADR-2: WebSocket over SSE (existing infra, bidirectional). ADR-3: Agentic loop over text-only (user expects LLM to act). ADR-4: Env vars for API keys (security, 12-factor). ADR-5: JSONB for multi-file skills (simple, matches patterns). ADR-6: Redis for sessions (fast, ephemeral, TTL).
alternatives: Per-module endpoints
SSE streaming
Text-only with Apply button
DB-stored API keys
Separate skill_files table
PostgreSQL for sessions
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED all 6 ADRs: WebSocket over SSE, global endpoint, agentic loop, env vars, JSONB skills, Redis sessions.

decisions.issue: Scope Creep — AI everywhere before core works
decisions.recommendation: Strict phasing via feature flags: Skills first (O-011), verify it works, then expand to one more module
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 22 (HIGH). Cascade risk: scope creep → less testing → more hallucinations → more cost → more injection surface. Root cause: trying to do too much too fast. Feature flags enforce discipline.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Feature flags default: skills=true, rest=false. Each module enabled ONLY after previous one verified. Communicate timeline to user. Resist pressure to enable all at once.
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Feature flags + strict phasing enforced.

decisions.issue: Prompt Injection via user input and uploaded files
decisions.recommendation: Defense in depth: JSON Schema validation on tool calls, permission system limits blast radius, file content sanitization, all tool calls logged for audit
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 22 (HIGH). LLM processes user text and uploaded files — both vectors for injection. Tool-use amplifies risk because injected instructions can trigger real API calls. Permission system is the primary defense: even if injection succeeds, LLM can only do what permissions allow.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Strict JSON Schema validation on every tool call. 2. Entity scoping: LLM can only modify context entity by default. 3. File upload sanitization (strip system prompt patterns). 4. All tool calls logged with full args for audit. 5. Permission system as blast radius limiter.
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Defense in depth: validation + scoping + sanitization + logging + permissions.

decisions.issue: Token Cost Explosion from agentic loops
decisions.recommendation: Per-session soft/hard token limits, model selection (Haiku for simple ops), cost dashboard in UI
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 19 (HIGH). Agentic loops with tool-use can use 10-50x tokens vs single-shot. Cost accumulates over days/weeks. Detectable via token tracking. Cobra effect: hard cutoffs frustrate users → break into many sessions → more overhead.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Soft limit with warning (configurable, default 50k tokens per session). 2. Hard limit with option to continue (user explicit click). 3. Model selection per contract (Haiku for suggestions, Sonnet for editing). 4. Cost dashboard in settings page. 5. Per-session token counter visible in chat UI.
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Soft/hard limits + model selection + cost dashboard.

decisions.issue: LLM Tool Call Hallucination — wrong entity modified
decisions.recommendation: Strict JSON Schema validation, entity scoping, tool call preview in UI, undo via versioning
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 19 (HIGH). Claude's tool-use is reliable but not perfect. Damage from wrong tool call is immediate (entity modified). Permission system + entity scoping are primary defenses. Tool call visibility in chat lets user catch errors before they cascade.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. JSON Schema validation rejects malformed tool calls. 2. Entity scoping: tools operate on context entity only (e.g., current skill). 3. Tool calls visible in chat UI with args preview. 4. Entity versioning provides undo capability. 5. Optional: user confirmation before destructive operations (delete, status change).
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Validation + scoping + preview + versioning + confirmation.

decisions.issue: O-009 Skills Management completion strategy — targeted fixes vs full polish vs defer
decisions.recommendation: Option A: Targeted fixes (3-5 tasks). The module is ~75% done with core CRUD, linting, promotion, and DB schema all implemented. A focused push on task-skill integration UI, usage tracking, scope-based filtering, and TESLint Docker verification closes all 7 KRs. Defer enhancements (inline annotations, markdown preview, .teslintrc editor) to post-testing iteration.
decisions.reasoning: Codebase analysis confirms: backend router (829 lines), TESLint service (176 lines), frontmatter parser (237 lines), PostgreSQL migration, frontend pages/editor/store all exist. KR-1 (API endpoints) and KR-2 (PostgreSQL schema) are 100% done. KR-3 through KR-7 are 60-90% done. Option A has highest ROI: minimum tasks, maximum KR closure. Option B (full polish) delays other objectives (O-005/O-006/O-007) and risks over-engineering before real usage. Option C (defer) creates test debt.
findings: KR-1 (Backend API): 6/6 endpoints + bonus (import/export/bulk lint/categories) — DONE
KR-2 (PostgreSQL schema): Global skills table with indexes — DONE
KR-3 (Frontend CRUD): List + editor + create pages exist — ~90% (missing: usage display, markdown preview)
KR-4 (Promotion system): 3-gate validation + force override + history — ~95% (needs: visual badge for force-promoted)
KR-5 (Categorization): 10 categories + filters + sidebar — ~85% (missing: scope/tag grouping toggles)
KR-6 (Task integration): Backend skill field exists, frontend dropdown partial — ~50% (missing: scope filtering, usage tracking increment, 'used by' display)
KR-7 (TESLint): Backend subprocess works, frontend lint button exists — ~80% (missing: Docker verification, custom config UI)
open_questions: Is TaskForm skill dropdown functional or a stub?
Is usage_count actually incremented on task creation?
Is TESLint installed in the Docker image?
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED Option A: Targeted fixes (3-5 tasks). User accepted.

decisions.issue: TESLint not installed in Docker container — all lint/promote endpoints fail
decisions.recommendation: Verify TESLint in requirements.txt and Dockerfile. Add health check endpoint. Test lint endpoint in Docker.
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 23 (HIGHEST). TESLint integration (teslint.py) runs subprocess python -m teslint. If the package is not in requirements.txt or Docker image, every call to POST /skills/{id}/lint and POST /skills/{id}/promote returns a 500 error. High velocity (instant failure) and low detectability (no one has tested in container yet). Cascade: if TESLint fails, force-promote becomes the only path, undermining quality gates.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Check requirements.txt for teslint package. 2. Add to Dockerfile if missing. 3. Add GET /health/teslint endpoint returning version info. 4. Test lint endpoint in running Docker container.
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Will verify during implementation tasks.

decisions.issue: Task-skill integration incomplete — skill dropdown may be non-functional
decisions.recommendation: Code review TaskForm.tsx to verify skill dropdown works end-to-end with search, scope filtering, and proper API calls.
decisions.reasoning: Composite score 14 (MEDIUM-HIGH). This is the key integration point — skills are only useful if tasks can reference them. The commit message mentions 'Task form dropdown + skill detail link' but scope-based filtering and usage tracking may be stubs. If incomplete, usage_count stays at 0 for all skills, making the tracking KR hollow. Cascade: R2 → R3 (no task integration means no usage count).
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Read TaskForm.tsx and verify skill dropdown implementation. 2. Verify tasks router increments usage_count on task creation with skill_id. 3. Test end-to-end: create task with skill → verify skill usage_count incremented. 4. Add scope-based filtering if missing.
decisions.resolution_notes: Mitigation plan accepted. Will verify and fix during implementation tasks.

decisions.issue: Task-Skill integration pattern: how tasks reference global skills
decisions.recommendation: Use skill_id (S-NNN string) in task, with backend validation that skill exists and is ACTIVE or DEPRECATED (with warning). Frontend: async select with search and scope-based filtering. Show matching skills first in 'Recommended' section, then 'All skills'.
decisions.reasoning: Consistent with Forge entity ID pattern (T-NNN, D-NNN, K-NNN). String ID is human-readable and works across JSON/PostgreSQL modes. Scope-based suggestion (not strict filter) balances relevance with discoverability. Backend already has skill field in task schema.
alternatives: Store full SKILL.md content in task (rejected — duplication)
Use skill name instead of ID (rejected — not unique)
Integer FK (rejected — inconsistent with entity ID pattern)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. S-NNN string ID, scope-based suggestion with Recommended + All sections, backend validation on task creation.

decisions.issue: Layout CSS variable scheme and padding strategy for 3-column architecture
decisions.recommendation: Define CSS custom properties: --topnav-height (48px), --left-panel-width (auto, driven by content), --right-sidebar-width (420px default). Remove p-6 from root layout <main> — let each page/layout manage its own padding. This eliminates the -m-6 hack in project layout. All calc(100vh-X) patterns must reference --topnav-height instead of hardcoded values. Use CSS Grid for 3-column body: grid-template-columns: auto 1fr auto.
decisions.reasoning: The current p-6 on root <main> forces the project layout to use -m-6 counter-hack. This fragile coupling breaks with any layout restructuring. CSS variables enable all viewport-height calculations to automatically adjust when top nav height changes. Removing root padding gives each page control over its own spacing while eliminating the hack. 4 files with calc(100vh-X) are known: skills/new, skills/[id], execution/[taskId], BottomPanel.
alternatives: Keep p-6 and adjust -m-6 hack (rejected — fragile)
Use Tailwind theme extend for layout sizes (rejected — dynamic resize needs inline styles)
Use CSS Container Queries (rejected — overkill, browser support concerns)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. CSS variables + remove root p-6 + page-managed padding.

decisions.issue: Relationship between scopes (multi-valued, route-derived) and context_type (single-valued, entity-based)
decisions.recommendation: Scopes and context_type are ADDITIVE concepts: (1) context_type + context_id = what entity the LLM is focused on (singular, e.g., 'skill' + 'SK-001'), (2) scopes = what areas the user has enabled for tool availability (can be multiple, e.g., ['skills', 'tasks']). Frontend scopes map to backend context_types via a shared mapping: scope 'skills' -> context_type 'skill', scope 'tasks' -> context_type 'task', etc. (plural-to-singular). get_tools() is extended to accept context_types: list[str] (plural), returning the UNION of tools from all matching types. disabled_capabilities maps to tool names to exclude.
decisions.reasoning: context_type already works well for entity-level focus — the LLM needs to know it's looking at SK-001. Scopes add a higher-level concept of 'what operations are available' which can span multiple entity types (user adds 'knowledge' scope while on 'skills' page). Making scopes additive to context_type preserves backward compatibility while enabling the new multi-scope capability. The plural-to-singular mapping is a simple const object, not a complex system.
alternatives: Replace context_type with scopes entirely (rejected — loses entity-level focus)
Make scopes === context_type with different naming (rejected — scopes can be multi-valued, context_type cannot)
Use scopes only on frontend, convert to context_type on backend (rejected — loses multi-scope tool union)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Scopes additive to context_type. Frontend: scopes[] -> mapped to context_types[] via const. Backend: get_tools(context_types: list[str], disabled_tools: list[str]).

decisions.issue: Tool gap: only skills scope has dedicated backend tools, other scopes have generic read-only tools
decisions.recommendation: Capability contracts for non-skills scopes mark write/delete operations as available: false with UI showing 'Coming soon' label. READ operations use generic tools (listEntities, searchEntities, getEntity) which work for ALL entity types. This provides immediate value (scope-aware read capabilities) while clearly communicating future work. The 'Coming soon' label is better than hiding operations — it shows users what will be possible. Backend tools for other entity types (createTask, updateObjective, etc.) can be added incrementally in future objectives.
decisions.reasoning: Building 15+ dedicated CRUD tools for 5 entity types would triple O-013's scope and delay the entire AI Sidebar feature. The generic read tools already provide useful capabilities (list tasks, search knowledge, get decision details). Write/delete tools for non-skills scopes are a logical follow-up but not blocking for the initial release. The 'Coming soon' approach sets user expectations correctly and creates a natural backlog.
alternatives: Create all tools now (rejected — scope creep, delays O-013 by weeks)
Hide unavailable operations entirely (rejected — users won't know what's planned)
Show all as available but let them fail (rejected — bad UX, LLM wastes turns)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. available: false + 'Coming soon' for write/delete on non-skills scopes. READ via generic tools. Tools can be added incrementally.

decisions.issue: Permission model: per-user vs global LLM permissions for capability contracts
decisions.recommendation: Use GLOBAL LLM config permissions (from GET /api/v1/llm/config) for capability contract enforcement. No per-user permissions exist yet — the system has a single global LLMConfig with module-level read/write/delete. The 'No permission' state in capability contracts reflects what the system admin has configured globally, not per-user restrictions. When per-user auth is implemented in the future, extend this to intersect user role permissions with LLM config permissions.
decisions.reasoning: The current auth system (auth.py) has roles (admin/user/readonly) but no per-user module-level permissions. LLM permissions live in _global/llm_config.json as a flat structure. Building per-user LLM permissions would require: (1) user-to-permissions mapping table, (2) auth middleware changes, (3) permission management UI, (4) per-user config storage — all significant work that is outside O-013's scope. The global permission model works correctly for single-admin scenarios which is the current deployment model.
alternatives: Build per-user permissions now (rejected — requires auth system overhaul)
Map user roles to permission tiers automatically (considered — could add later as lightweight extension)
Ignore permissions entirely for MVP (rejected — 'No permission' state is core to capability contract UX)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. Global LLM config permissions. Per-user can be layered on later via role-to-permission mapping.

decisions.issue: Minimum content area width with both sidebars expanded (left 224px + right 420px = 644px consumed)
decisions.recommendation: Set minimum content area width to 500px. Auto-collapse left panel first when viewport width < left + 500 + right. Right sidebar defaults to COLLAPSED in O-012 (populated by O-014) — on first use, only left panel + content visible. When both panels expanded: on 1280px screen = 616px content (acceptable), on 1024px = auto-collapse left panel. Add hidden md:block on left and right columns for basic mobile safety. No full responsive design yet.
decisions.reasoning: Common laptop screens are 1280-1440px. With left panel (224px) + right sidebar (420px), content gets 636-796px — tight but workable for entity lists and forms. The critical scenario is 1024px screens where 500px minimum triggers auto-collapse. Right sidebar defaulting to collapsed means first-time experience is normal (left + content only). Users explicitly expand the AI sidebar when they want it. Auto-collapse priority: left panel first (less critical than AI chat), then right sidebar.
alternatives: No minimum, let content crush (rejected — breaks existing layouts)
Force right sidebar to overlay instead of pushing content (rejected — overlays hide content)
Reduce right sidebar default to 360px (considered — could reduce if 420px proves too wide in practice)
decisions.resolution_notes: ACCEPTED. 500px min content. Auto-collapse left panel first. Right sidebar collapsed by default. Basic md:block responsive safety.

decisions.issue: O-011 remaining gaps: temp file storage strategy for chat file uploads
decisions.recommendation: Redis with 1h TTL for temp file content storage
decisions.reasoning: User selected Redis over in-memory dict or filesystem. Redis survives process restarts, has native TTL support, and is already in the stack (Redis Stack 7.4). Key pattern: chat:file:{uuid}, auto-expires after 1h.
alternatives: In-memory dict with manual TTL cleanup
Filesystem /tmp with cron cleanup

decisions.issue: O-011 remaining gaps: LLM tool strategy — replace or extend
decisions.recommendation: Add instantiateACTemplate + previewSkill alongside existing 8 tools (10 total)
decisions.reasoning: User chose to keep listSkillFiles + getSkillFileContent (useful file management tools) and add the 2 originally-specified tools. More capabilities for the LLM, no removal of working tools.
alternatives: Replace listSkillFiles + getSkillFileContent with the 2 new tools (keep 8)

decisions.issue: Skills storage model: JSON-blob vs file-based directories
decisions.recommendation: File-based directories with _config.json per skill
decisions.reasoning: Obecny model trzyma cały markdown + pliki jako stringi w jednym skills.json. To uniemożliwia sensowne git diffy, normalne edytowanie plików, i skaluje się źle. Katalogowy model daje: git-friendly diffy, normalne pliki na dysku, lazy loading (nie trzeba wczytywać wszystkich skills naraz), naturalną strukturę.
findings: skills.json rośnie z każdym skillem (treść + pliki do 100KB x 50)
Escapowany markdown w JSON jest nieczytelny w diffach
Bundled files jako stringi w resources.files[].content — duplikacja + brak normalnego dostępu
Frontmatter SKILL.md już zawiera name/description/version — duplikacja z polami JSON
StorageAdapter protocol wymaga rozszerzenia o operacje katalogowe

options.name: Hybrid (metadata w JSON, pliki na dysku)

decisions.issue: Git sync model dla skills repo
decisions.recommendation: Osobny git clone w _global/skills/ — Forge zarządza pull/push przez API endpointy
decisions.reasoning: Subtree/submodule dodają złożoność. Prosty clone z ręcznym pull/push daje pełną kontrolę. ForgePrime periodic auto-pull opcjonalny. User decyduje kiedy pushuje.
alternatives: Git subtree (skomplikowany sync)
Git submodule (problematyczne w praktyce)

decisions.issue: Nazewnictwo katalogów skills
decisions.recommendation: Katalog = nazwa skilla (code-review/), _config.json wewnątrz trzyma id: S-001 do wewnętrznych referencji
decisions.reasoning: Nazwy jako katalogi są czytelne w git, unikalne w repo, i działają jak package names. S-NNN jako katalogi byłyby nieczytelne. ID w _config.json pozwala na wewnętrzne referencje.
alternatives: S-NNN jako katalogi (nieczytelne)
UUID (jeszcze gorsze)

decisions.issue: Source of truth podział: _config.json vs SKILL.md frontmatter
decisions.recommendation: Frontmatter SKILL.md = source of truth dla name, description, version, allowed-tools. _config.json = source of truth dla categories, tags, status, evals, promotion_history, scopes, sync flag.
decisions.reasoning: SKILL.md z frontmatterem jest self-contained i przenośne (można wziąć plik i użyć w innym systemie). _config.json trzyma metadane specyficzne dla Forge, które nie mają sensu w przenośnym formacie.
alternatives: Wszystko w _config.json (frontmatter dekoracyjny)
Wszystko w frontmatter (za dużo metadanych w YAML)
decisions.resolution_notes: Frontmatter SKILL.md jest source of truth dla name/description/version/allowed-tools. _config.json trzyma resztę (categories, tags, status, evals, promotion_history, scopes, sync).

decisions.issue: Lokalność skills: local-only vs synced
decisions.recommendation: Flaga sync: true/false w _config.json. Domyślnie local. User explicite może oznaczyć skill jako synced i pushować do remote.
decisions.reasoning: Nie każdy skill ma trafić do publicznego repo. Lokalne prototypy, WIP, prywatne skills — muszą żyć bez synca. Flaga sync pozwala na oba tryby.
alternatives: Wszystko synced (brak prywatności)
Osobny katalog local/ vs synced/ (komplikuje strukturę)

decisions.issue: Concurrent access do plików skills bez atomicznych operacji JSON
decisions.recommendation: Per-skill file locking (lockfile per katalog) + git jako backup
decisions.reasoning: Obecny model ma atomiczne JSON writes. Plikowy model wymaga locking per skill. Dwa procesy edytujące ten sam skill mogą nadpisać sobie pliki.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Implementacja per-skill asyncio lock (jak obecny _get_lock ale per skill directory). Git commit po każdej zmianie daje rollback.
decisions.resolution_notes: Na razie wystarczy obecny mechanizm lockowania. Wrócimy do tematu jeśli pojawią się problemy z concurrent access.

decisions.issue: Merge conflicts przy git pull gdy lokalne i remote zmiany na tym samym skill
decisions.recommendation: Detect conflicts, present to user, nie auto-resolve
decisions.reasoning: Git merge conflicts na SKILL.md lub _config.json mogą być trudne do auto-resolve. Lepiej wykryć i pokazać userowi niż cicho nadpisać.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Git pull z --no-commit, sprawdź conflicts, jeśli są — pokaż w UI i pozwól userowi rozwiązać. Alternatywnie: git stash przed pull + reapply.
decisions.resolution_notes: Przy git pull nadpisywać lokalne zmiany tym co jest w repo (git reset --hard origin/main po fetch). Remote repo jest source of truth dla synced skills.

decisions.issue: API routing: skill_id (S-NNN) vs skill name jako identyfikator
decisions.recommendation: API operuje po nazwie skilla (slug). Nazwa musi być unikalna. Katalog = nazwa = API identifier. S-NNN odpada.
decisions.reasoning: Skoro katalog = nazwa, to naturalne jest operowanie po nazwie w API. Wymuszenie unikalności nazwy rozwiązuje konflikty. S-NNN nie jest potrzebne gdy nazwa jest unikalnym identyfikatorem.
alternatives: Zachować S-NNN i mapować do katalogu (dodatkowa warstwa indirekcji)

decisions.issue: Synchronizacja name między UI a SKILL.md frontmatter
decisions.recommendation: Walidacja przy save: name w UI musi zgadzać się z name w frontmatter SKILL.md. Jeśli user zmieni name w UI — auto-update frontmattera. Jeśli zmieni frontmatter — auto-update pola w UI. Jedno źródło prawdy (frontmatter) z bidirectional sync.
decisions.reasoning: Rozbieżność name między UI a frontmatterem prowadziłaby do niespójności. Frontmatter jest source of truth, ale UI musi to odzwierciedlać i wymuszać spójność.

decisions.issue: Czy SKILL.md jest wymagany przy tworzeniu skilla
decisions.recommendation: SKILL.md jest wymagany. Przy tworzeniu nowego skilla auto-generowany jest minimalny SKILL.md z frontmatterem (name, version, description). User nie może zapisać skilla bez SKILL.md.
decisions.reasoning: SKILL.md jest source of truth dla name/description. Bez niego skill nie ma tożsamości. Auto-generowanie minimalizuje friction przy tworzeniu.

decisions.issue: Gdzie trzymać indeks skills do szybkiego wyszukiwania
decisions.recommendation: DB jako cache/indeks (name, categories, tags, status, path). Pliki na dysku = source of truth. Upsert do DB przy create/edit/sync. Resync z katalogów naprawia rozbieżności.
decisions.reasoning: Skanowanie katalogów + parsowanie frontmattera przy każdym GET /skills jest wolne przy 50+ skills. DB indeks daje szybkie filtrowanie, search, sortowanie. DB nie jest source of truth — jeśli się rozjedzie, resync z plików naprawia.
alternatives: In-memory cache (ginie po restarcie)
Tylko pliki, bez indeksu (wolne listing)

decisions.issue: PageManifest schema design — what structure can drive both React rendering and AI serialization from a single definition
decisions.recommendation: Hierarchical manifest with typed elements, declarative actions referencing API endpoints, semantic layout areas, conditional visibility, and data source bindings. Elements are the atomic units. Each element declares its type, label, position, action(s), and conditions. Data sources bind to API endpoints and provide live rows. Scopes are derived automatically by extracting module names from endpoint paths.
findings: Current UI has 4 layout patterns: List (7 pages), Dashboard (2), Settings (1), Detail (1) — manifest must handle all 4
7 distinct card types (Task, Objective, Idea, Decision, Knowledge, Guideline, Skill) — each is a composition of badges, text, actions — decomposable into manifest elements
All forms use FormDrawer pattern with consistent field types: text, textarea, select, multi-select, entity-ref, dynamic-list, checkbox — these become manifest form element types
Status transitions are per-entity-type with specific valid transitions — manifest needs a state machine or transition map
Filter pattern is universal: status dropdown + optional category/search — manifest standardizes this as a filter-bar element
Scopes currently hardcoded in useScopeResolver via regex — manifest endpoints make this automatic: /api/tasks/* -> tasks scope
capabilities.ts defines 70+ capabilities statically — manifest replaces this with dynamic capability derivation from element actions
Live state capture is feasible: each element type has a known serializable state (select -> current value, table -> visible rows, form -> field values)

options.name: Flat element list with layout hints
Hierarchical elements with typed containers
pros: AI serialization follows tree with indentation

open_questions: Should manifest files live alongside page components or in a central registry?
How to handle entity-specific card rendering (7 card types) — polymorphic element or dedicated card-type per entity?
What is the right granularity for AI serialization — full page snapshot every message vs. incremental deltas?

decisions.issue: Generic renderer may produce lower UX quality than hand-crafted components
decisions.recommendation: Allow manifest elements to specify custom renderer overrides — fallback to generic, but skilled developers can provide optimized components for specific element types
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1) Start with one page (Tasks list) as proof of concept. 2) Compare visual quality side-by-side with current implementation. 3) Allow renderer: custom component name as escape hatch in manifest elements. 4) Iterate on generic renderer until quality gap is negligible.

decisions.issue: Serialized page context may exceed LLM token limits for data-heavy pages
decisions.recommendation: Implement tiered serialization: structure always included, data rows truncated with summary, form values always included
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1) Measure serialized size for each page type. 2) Set max 4000 tokens for page context. 3) Truncate data rows to first 10 + total count. 4) AI can request full data via tool call if needed.

decisions.issue: How should scopes be derived from manifest API endpoints
decisions.recommendation: Extract module name from endpoint path segment: /api/{module}/... or /projects/{slug}/{module}/... → scope = module. Deduplicate. This replaces the static SCOPE_TO_CONTEXT_TYPE mapping and useScopeResolver route rules entirely.
decisions.reasoning: Current system maps URLs to scopes via regex (fragile, manual maintenance). Manifest already declares all API endpoints in data_source and action definitions. Extracting scope from endpoint path is deterministic, automatic, and always synchronized with what the page actually does.
alternatives: Explicit scopes field on manifest (still manual, but at manifest level)
Derive from tool names instead of endpoints (couples to tool registry)

decisions.issue: UI Manifest renderer creates triple maintenance: manifest schema + 14 renderers + serializer. Abstraction too heavy, expressiveness limited by schema, CustomElement escape hatch proves inadequacy.
decisions.recommendation: Replace manifest-driven rendering with lightweight AI Annotations (useAIElement hooks) on existing React components. Keep deriveScopes, serializer concept, action types. Drop ManifestRenderer, element renderers, page manifest files.
decisions.reasoning: Manifest promised single source of truth but delivered 3 places to update per UI change. Annotations achieve same AI understanding with zero rendering overhead. Components self-describe via hooks, page provider collects registrations, serializer produces AI text from collected data.

decisions.issue: Windows subprocess proc.kill() only kills direct child (cmd.exe wrapper), orphaning Node.js children
decisions.recommendation: Implemented _kill_process_tree() using taskkill /F /T /PID on Windows for full process tree cleanup. Falls back to proc.kill() on non-Windows platforms.
decisions.reasoning: Claude Code CLI binary on Windows is claude.cmd (npm batch wrapper) that spawns Node.js. proc.kill() sends TerminateProcess only to the direct child, leaving Node.js orphaned. This would leak processes on every timeout or cancellation. taskkill /T kills the entire process tree.
alternatives: Use proc.kill() everywhere (leaks on Windows)
Use psutil for process tree management (adds dependency)

decisions.issue: Session map (forge_session_id -> cc_session_id) is an unbounded dict that grows indefinitely in long-running server
decisions.recommendation: Replaced plain dict with OrderedDict and LRU eviction at 1000 entries via _update_session_map() helper
decisions.reasoning: In a long-running web server, sessions accumulate without limit. OrderedDict with move_to_end + popitem(last=False) provides O(1) LRU eviction without external dependencies. 1000 entries is generous for concurrent sessions.
alternatives: Use functools.lru_cache (doesn't fit key-value pattern)
Use TTL-based cache (adds complexity)
No limit (memory leak)

decisions.issue: How should BottomPanel children (LlmMonitor, EventStream) receive project slug?
decisions.recommendation: Extract slug from URL pathname via regex in BottomPanel, pass as prop to all children. Use usePathname() from next/navigation for reactivity.
decisions.reasoning: useParams() only works inside matching route segments. BottomPanel is in root layout, outside [slug]. Three options evaluated: prop-based slug (recommended), global WS manager (over-engineering), disable on non-project (reduced functionality).
findings: useParams() returns {} in root layout - confirmed root cause of LlmMonitor/EventStream failure
usePathname() from next/navigation works in root layout and is reactive
Graceful degradation: when slug is null, show disabled state with navigation hint

decisions.issue: How should Debug tab in AI Sidebar handle streaming token data without re-render cascades?
decisions.recommendation: ref+subscription pattern: accumulate tokens in useRef, parse content_block boundaries, throttle React state updates to 100ms intervals via requestAnimationFrame.
decisions.reasoning: L-018 documents the exact infinite re-render pattern from context setState in useEffect. Tokens arrive at 20-50/second - per-token setState causes 20-50 re-renders/second. ref+subscription gives O(1) renders per 100ms regardless of token rate.
alternatives: useState per token (known to cause infinite loops - L-018)
Web Worker for parsing (over-engineering)
Virtual list (unnecessary - 3-10 blocks per response)

decisions.issue: JSON syntax highlighting: react-json-view vs custom lightweight highlighter
decisions.recommendation: Build custom lightweight JSON highlighter (<5KB) using regex-based tokenization and CSS classes.
decisions.reasoning: react-json-view is 150KB+ gzipped, unmaintained since 2023. Custom highlighter gives full control over styling, minimal bundle impact. Trade-off: no interactive collapse/expand per node, which is acceptable for debug context where full visibility is preferred.
alternatives: react-json-view (150KB+, unmaintained)
prism.js JSON grammar (30KB, maintained)
No highlighting, just monospace (poor readability)

decisions.issue: Error display strategy: where to enrich errors and how to render them consistently
decisions.recommendation: Two-layer approach: (1) ApiError class in api.ts with status/url/method/responseExcerpt, (2) shared ErrorDetail component used by all debug views.
decisions.reasoning: Current errors are generic because chatStore only extracts .message from caught errors. Enriching at api.ts level gives all consumers access to full context. Shared ErrorDetail component ensures consistent rendering across ApiInspector, LlmMonitor, Chat, and Debug tab.
findings: debugStore already receives full error context (status, url, method, body) - the data IS there
chatStore catches errors and replaces streaming message with [Error: {message}] - loses context
LlmMonitor silently swallows errors (catch {}) - relies on ApiInspector to show failures

options.name: Backend structured errors only
cons: Requires backend changes across all endpoints

decisions.issue: Infinite re-render from Debug tab WS subscriptions
decisions.recommendation: Use ref+subscription pattern (proven in L-018). Accumulate tokens in useRef, throttle state updates to 100ms.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Use useRef for token buffer, requestAnimationFrame for throttled updates. Test with large responses (1000+ tokens). Profile with React DevTools.

decisions.issue: WS chat.token event format may lack content_block type metadata for structured stream view
decisions.recommendation: Inspect backend WS emission code. If metadata missing, extend backend to include content_block_type in chat.token events.
decisions.mitigation_plan: Check forge-api LLM router WS emission. If only raw text deltas sent, add content_block_type field. Small backend change, high impact on frontend.

decisions.issue: Scope creep from large appetite + 6 KRs across bugs, features, and redesign
decisions.recommendation: Phase deliveries: Phase 1 (bug fixes - KR-1, KR-2, KR-4), Phase 2 (new features - KR-3, KR-5), Phase 3 (visual polish - KR-6).
decisions.mitigation_plan: Ship Phase 1 first (quick wins, fixes broken functionality). Then Phase 2 (new Debug tab, request inspector). Then Phase 3 (design tokens, visual consistency). Each phase is independently valuable.

decisions.issue: Debug panel may expose sensitive data (API keys in headers, auth tokens in request bodies)
decisions.recommendation: Implement header redaction in ApiInspector: mask Authorization header values, redact any field matching /key|token|secret|password/i patterns.
decisions.reasoning: API Inspector shows full request/response including headers. Adding request params display (KR-5) increases exposure surface. Debug data is in-memory only (not persisted), but visible on screen.
alternatives: No redaction (security risk)
Opt-in sensitive mode (complexity)
Only redact in production (debug console is dev-only anyway)

decisions.issue: How should AI session authorization work? Need scope-enforced, per-session tokens that prevent unauthorized tool calls even if LLM attempts them.
decisions.recommendation: Redis Scope Snapshot: store allowed scopes per session in Redis, validate every tool call server-side. Stateless, fast lookup, invalidate on scope change.
decisions.reasoning: JWT approach was considered but adds complexity (signing, refresh, revocation). Redis snapshot is simpler: on session create or scope change, write allowed_scopes set to Redis key with session TTL. Tool executor checks membership before executing. Already have Redis infrastructure.
findings: Current system has NO auth on chat endpoint - any tool call is executed if in registry
Permission engine exists (3-layer merge) but is not session-bound
Redis Scope Snapshot: O(1) lookup, atomic invalidation, natural TTL expiry
JWT alternative: adds signing/verification overhead, harder to revoke mid-session
open_questions: Should scope snapshot include tool-level granularity or just module scopes?

decisions.issue: What progressive context strategy should AI use? Need lightweight always-on context + on-demand detail without exceeding token budgets.
decisions.recommendation: Hybrid: static app map (~500 tokens) + full current page context (from annotations) + on-demand getToolContract(toolName) for detailed parameter schemas.
decisions.reasoning: Full tool registry dump would be 3000+ tokens. Static map gives overview of all capabilities. Page context (already implemented as SKILL-like format) gives actionable detail for current page. getToolContract bridges the gap when AI needs parameter details for a tool not on current page.
findings: Current page context (SKILL-like format) averages 300-800 tokens - fits within budget
Full tool registry has ~50 tools, dumping all schemas would be ~3000 tokens
App map (module -> tool names only, no schemas) fits in ~500 tokens
getToolContract returns full JSON Schema for one tool - ~100-200 tokens per call

options.name: Hybrid (map + page + on-demand)
cons: Extra tool call for detailed schemas

options.name: Page Context Only
cons: AI blind to tools not on current page

decisions.issue: How should getToolContract work? AI needs to discover available tools and get parameter schemas on demand.
decisions.recommendation: Two-Tool Pattern: listAvailableTools() returns tool names grouped by module (filtered by session scopes), getToolContract(toolName) returns full JSON Schema + description + examples for one tool.
decisions.reasoning: Single tool would either return too much (all schemas) or require AI to guess tool names. Two-tool pattern: list first (cheap, ~200 tokens), then get detail for specific tool (100-200 tokens). Naturally supports scope filtering - only list tools the session has access to.
findings: ToolRegistry already has all data needed: name, description, parameters (JSON Schema), scope
listAvailableTools can filter by session scopes from Redis snapshot
getToolContract can return full ToolDef metadata minus handler function
Both tools should be always-available (no scope requirement - meta-tools)

options.name: Single getAllTools
cons: Either too verbose or too terse

decisions.issue: How should scope changes propagate from UI toggles to active AI sessions?
decisions.recommendation: localStorage Event Listener: UI writes scopes to localStorage, AISidebar listens for storage events, invalidates Redis scope snapshot, and notifies active agent loop.
decisions.reasoning: Already use localStorage for scope persistence. Storage events fire cross-tab. Agent loop can check scope validity at each iteration boundary. No WebSocket needed for scope sync - only for chat streaming.
findings: Scopes already persisted in localStorage by AISidebar
Storage events fire on changes from other tabs/components
Agent loop has natural iteration boundaries for scope recheck
Redis scope snapshot can be atomically replaced on scope change

decisions.issue: Where should the app map (overview of all AI capabilities) be generated?
decisions.recommendation: Backend-Generated from ToolRegistry: endpoint returns tool names grouped by module, filtered by session scopes. Cached in memory, invalidated on registry changes.
decisions.reasoning: ToolRegistry is the single source of truth for available tools. Backend generation ensures consistency with actual tool availability. Frontend would need to duplicate tool knowledge. Memory caching is fine - registry changes only on deploy.
findings: ToolRegistry.create_default_registry() has all tool metadata
Tools already have scope field for grouping
App map is static per deployment - safe to cache
